one_line_takeaway: A 32-paper SoK that catalogs how the four classical ML attack classes (backdoor, membership inference, adversarial, inversion) intersect with machine unlearning via four relationship types (attack-on-MU, MU-as-defense, attack-as-evaluation, attack-as-verification), arguing that unlearning is a double-edged privacy tool whose defenses and verification methods remain underdeveloped.
key_finding: The authors surface a consistent 'double-edged' pattern: the same unlearning machinery that enforces data deletion also creates exploitable structure. Concretely, they find four recurring relationship types between ML attacks and MU and show that every one of the four studied attack classes appears on both the offense and the evaluation/defense side. Backdoor attacks dominate the corpus (used both AS attacks on MU and AS verification/evaluation tools), MIA is overwhelmingly used as an evaluation metric for whether forgetting succeeded (the intuition: if an MIA still flags an 'unlearned' sample as a member, unlearning failed), adversarial-attack work is sparse and split between attacking MU and using MU as a robustness defense, and inversion attacks are the least studied. They conclude that defenses and, especially, verification methodologies for MU are underdeveloped, and flag open problems: privacy-preserving unlearning in federated settings, MU for large/transformer models (GPT/BERT scale), verifiable/auditable unlearning (e.g., blockchain-backed certification), and legal/ethical alignment with GDPR.
core_contribution: A Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) that maps the intersection between classical Machine Learning security/privacy attacks and Machine Unlearning (MU). The paper's OWN contribution is a two-axis taxonomy, not any new attack, defense, or algorithm. Axis 1 organizes the reviewed literature by four attack classes: Backdoor Attacks (BA), Membership Inference Attacks (MIA), Adversarial Attacks (AA), and Inversion Attacks (IA, split into Model Inversion and Gradient Inversion). Axis 2 is a novel relationship-based classification describing how each attack relates to MU: (i) the attack is perpetrated AGAINST an MU system, (ii) MU is used as a DEFENSE/erasing mechanism against the attack, (iii) the attack is used as an EVALUATION tool to demonstrate a new MU framework's efficacy, and (iv) the attack is used as a VERIFICATION tool to check that a provider actually deleted the data (e.g., MLaaS right-to-be-forgotten audits). The authors also compile MU background (exact vs. approximate, certified vs. empirical, instance/class/feature-level, centralized vs. federated), the standard MU key techniques (retraining, sharded/SISA, influence-function, knowledge-distillation, gradient-based, federated unlearning), and evaluation metrics (Forgetting Rate, Model Distance, Membership Inference Resistance, Accuracy Drop, Attack Success Rate, Unlearning Time). They position the SoK against prior surveys that cover MU fundamentals ([28,42,49,41]), federated unlearning ([34,39]), LLM unlearning ([1]), or MU privacy/threats alone ([5,35]), claiming to be the first to comprehensively link the four classical ML attack classes to MU.
threat_model: This is a survey; it does not propose a single threat model but catalogs the threat models present in the reviewed literature. Across the works it organizes attacker knowledge into black-box, gray-box, and white-box settings, and attack/defense timing into three phases relative to unlearning: Before Unlearning (BU), During Unlearning (DU), and Post Unlearning (PU). Representative catalogued threats (all attributable to CITED works, not to this paper): backdoors that exploit the unlearning request channel to activate or persist triggers (e.g., UBA-Inf [23] in MLaaS, backdoor-via-unlearning [33], FedMUA [6] in federated unlearning, poisoning attacks on certified unlearning [37]); MIAs that leverage the difference between original and unlearned models to detect residual membership (e.g., 'When Machine Unlearning Jeopardizes Privacy' [9]); adversarial attacks that submit malicious static/sequential deletion requests to derail selective forgetting [54]; and inversion attacks that reconstruct features or infer labels of unlearned data from parameter changes between the original and unlearned models (unlearning inversion attacks [22]). The paper's framing is that MU, intended to improve privacy compliance (GDPR/CCPA right-to-be-forgotten), simultaneously opens a new attack surface because the coexistence of pre- and post-unlearning model versions can itself leak information.
limitations: 1) No empirical validation of its own: findings are qualitative categorizations, with no meta-analysis, reproduction, or head-to-head benchmarking of the surveyed methods, so relative attack/defense effectiveness cannot be judged from this paper. 2) Small corpus (32 selected papers, 2020-2025), which limits coverage and may bias toward frequently cited venues. 3) Coverage skew: inversion and adversarial attacks are represented by only a handful of works each (4 and 4 respectively), so those branches of the taxonomy are thin. 4) Internal inconsistency in the framing of the relationship taxonomy: the Introduction and Section 3 describe FOUR relationship types, but the Conclusion enumerates them as 'three main perspectives' while still listing four items (i-iv). 5) LLM/generative-model unlearning is named as an open challenge but is largely out of scope of the reviewed corpus. 6) The paper does not propose or evaluate any concrete defense or verification protocol; it maps the space and defers benchmark-dataset analysis and legal/regulatory alignment to future work.

datasets_used: None of the authors' own — this is a survey with no experiments. Datasets appear only as attributes of cited works and are not aggregated or benchmarked in this paper.
quantitative_results: Corpus: 65 works analyzed, 32 papers selected, covering 2020-2025 (authors' own bibliometric figures).

Taxonomy size: 4 attack classes (Backdoor, Membership Inference, Adversarial, Inversion) x 4 attack-to-MU relationship types (attack against MU, MU as defense, attack as MU-framework evaluation, attack as MU-verification).
Reproduced field metric — Forgetting Rate: FR = 1 - (A_after / A_before), on removed data.
Reproduced field metric — Accuracy Drop: AD = |A_before - A_after| / A_before.
Other catalogued metrics (from the field, not measured here): Attack Success Rate (ASR), Unlearning Time (UT), Model Distance, Membership Inference Resistance.
Attack-knowledge and phase axes used to code the corpus: black-box / gray-box / white-box; and Before-Unlearning (BU), During-Unlearning (DU), Post-Unlearning (PU).
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines. Comparison is qualitative against PRIOR SURVEYS: MU fundamentals/taxonomy surveys [28,42,49,41]; federated-unlearning surveys [34,39]; LLM digital-forgetting survey [1]; MU privacy-risk survey [5]; MU threats-and-defenses survey [35]. The paper claims novelty as the first SoK to link all four classical ML attack classes to MU.
